title: Java Collections — Core Concepts
description: 20 questions covering ArrayList, HashMap, LinkedList, TreeMap, HashSet and their internals. Common in Java interviews.

questions.questionText: What is the default initial capacity of ArrayList in Java?
questions.explanation: ArrayList's default initial capacity is 10. When it exceeds this, it grows by 50% (newCapacity = oldCapacity + oldCapacity/2).

questions.questionText: What is the time complexity of get(index) in ArrayList?
questions.explanation: ArrayList is backed by a plain array. Index-based access is O(1) — it's a direct array element lookup with pointer arithmetic.

questions.questionText: Which of these does NOT allow duplicate elements?
questions.explanation: HashSet implements Set which guarantees uniqueness. ArrayList, LinkedList and ArrayDeque all implement List/Deque and allow duplicates.

questions.questionText: What is the time complexity of HashMap.get() in the average case?
questions.explanation: HashMap uses a hash function to directly locate the bucket. Average case is O(1). Worst case (all keys hash to the same bucket) is O(n), but Java 8+ converts buckets to balanced trees at 8 entries, giving O(log n) worst case.

questions.questionText: What happens to a HashMap when its load factor threshold is exceeded?
questions.optionC: It rehashes and doubles its capacity
questions.explanation: When (size > capacity × loadFactor), HashMap rehashes — it creates a new array of double capacity and re-inserts all entries. Default loadFactor is 0.75, default initial capacity is 16, so rehash triggers at 12 entries.

questions.questionText: What is the default load factor of HashMap?
questions.explanation: The default load factor is 0.75 — a balance between time and space cost. A lower value wastes memory; a higher value increases collision probability and degrades O(1) access.

questions.questionText: Which collection maintains insertion order AND allows duplicates?
questions.explanation: LinkedList (implementing List) maintains insertion order and allows duplicates. LinkedHashSet maintains insertion order but does NOT allow duplicates (it's a Set).

questions.questionText: What is the time complexity of add() at the beginning of LinkedList vs ArrayList?
questions.explanation: LinkedList add-first is O(1) — just update head pointer. ArrayList add-first is O(n) — must shift all elements right by one position to make room.

questions.questionText: Which Map implementation keeps keys in sorted order?
questions.explanation: TreeMap is backed by a Red-Black Tree and keeps keys in their natural sorted order (or by a provided Comparator). get/put/remove are all O(log n).

questions.questionText: Which of these is thread-safe without external synchronisation?
questions.explanation: ConcurrentHashMap uses segment-level locking (Java 7) and CAS operations (Java 8+) for thread safety without locking the whole map. ArrayList, HashMap, and LinkedList are NOT thread-safe.

questions.questionText: What does the poll() method of a Queue return when the queue is empty?
questions.explanation: poll() is the 'safe' retrieval method — returns null if empty. remove() throws NoSuchElementException if empty. This is a key difference to know for interviews.

questions.questionText: Which collection should you use to implement a stack in modern Java?
questions.explanation: ArrayDeque is preferred over java.util.Stack (which is a legacy class extending Vector, a synchronized class with unnecessary overhead). ArrayDeque as a stack offers O(1) push/pop with no synchronisation penalty.

questions.questionText: What does TreeSet use internally to store elements?
questions.explanation: TreeSet uses a Red-Black Tree (same as TreeMap internally — TreeSet is backed by a TreeMap with dummy values). This gives O(log n) add/remove/contains and keeps elements sorted.

questions.questionText: What happens if you try to insert null as a key in TreeMap?
questions.optionA: It stores null at the first position
questions.optionB: It stores null at the last position
questions.explanation: TreeMap requires keys to be Comparable (or a Comparator to be provided). Inserting null causes a NullPointerException during comparison. HashMap allows one null key because it skips hashing for null.

questions.questionText: Which of these is correct about the Iterator's remove() method?
questions.optionA: It cannot remove the last returned element
questions.optionB: It removes the element from the underlying collection safely
questions.optionD: It can only remove from the end
questions.explanation: Iterator.remove() is the safe way to remove an element during iteration. Calling collection.remove() directly while iterating causes ConcurrentModificationException. The Iterator's remove() updates the internal modCount correctly.
HashMap does not allow duplicate keys. The second put("a", 3) overwrites the first value (1) with 3. The map contains {"a"→3, "b"→2}, so size() returns 2.

questions.questionText: Which method returns a view of the portion of a List from fromIndex (inclusive) to toIndex (exclusive)?
questions.explanation: List.subList(fromIndex, toIndex) returns a view (not a copy) of the specified range. Changes to the subList are reflected in the original list. fromIndex is inclusive, toIndex is exclusive.

questions.questionText: What is the complexity of contains() on a HashSet?
questions.explanation: HashSet is backed by a HashMap. contains() calls the underlying HashMap.containsKey() which is O(1) average — just hash the element and check if it exists in that bucket.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between LinkedHashMap and HashMap?
questions.optionA: LinkedHashMap is thread-safe, HashMap is not
questions.optionB: LinkedHashMap maintains insertion order, HashMap does not
questions.optionD: LinkedHashMap allows null keys, HashMap does not
questions.explanation: LinkedHashMap maintains a doubly-linked list across entries to record insertion order (or access order if accessOrder=true). HashMap makes no ordering guarantees. Both allow null keys. Both are NOT thread-safe.

questions.questionText: Which is true about PriorityQueue in Java?
questions.optionB: It maintains elements in sorted order based on natural ordering or Comparator
questions.explanation: PriorityQueue uses a min-heap internally. poll() always returns the smallest element (by natural order or Comparator). It does NOT guarantee full sorted order for iteration — only the head is always the minimum. It allows duplicates but NOT null elements.
